accept: Stale-claim recovery policy is documented (in orchestration-design.md or the act skill)
If a heartbeat or timeout mechanism is selected, file separate implementation issues and link from this one
act doctor's role in orphan reconciliation is clarified — what does 'detect a stale claim' look like operationally
payload.description:
In Mode A, when a worker crashes mid-issue, the result is an in_progress claim that a human notices and reclaims-or-orphans based on judgment. In Mode B the orchestrator survives the worker's death and must have an algorithmic policy: how does it distinguish a slow worker from a dead one? When does it reclaim and reassign? What counts as evidence a worker is still alive?

The orchestration-design doc claims Mode A is the degenerate case of Mode B, but Mode A's "human notices" mechanism collapses into "orchestrator must algorithmically decide" in Mode B — and the doc doesn't specify the policy that Mode B requires and Mode A humanly improvises.

Options:
(a) Define a default policy any orchestrator follows — e.g., worker writes a heartbeat note every N seconds, orchestrator reclaims after 2N silence.
(b) Punt to orchestrator-side discretion (each orchestrator picks its own policy) with the implication that act doctor must support orphan reconciliation across whatever discretion the orchestrator showed.
(c) Hybrid — act ships a default policy and orchestrators can override.

Surfaced in the act-334e review (abstractions reviewer, 85% confidence — pointed out this is a real semantic asymmetry the doc claims doesn't exist).
payload.title: Document stale-claim recovery semantics for Mode B orchestrators
